overview: Quality of Service (QoS) manages Bandwidth, Delay, Jitter, and Loss so that latency-sensitive traffic like voice and video get the treatment they need on a congested or bandwidth-constrained link.
definitions: Bandwidth: link speed/capacity in bits per second.
Delay: time required for a packet to cross the network.
Jitter: variation in delay between received packets.
Loss: percentage of packets that fail to arrive.
Trust Boundary: the point in the network where QoS markings are trusted (ideally the ingress switch or IP phone).
core_concepts: Per-Hop Behavior (PHB) sequence: Classification -> Marking -> Forwarding decision -> Queuing/Scheduling.
Policing discards or re-marks excess traffic (ingress or egress); Shaping queues/delays packets to smooth them to a target rate (egress only).
protocol_operation: Device classifies it by matching header fields.
Packet is queued/scheduled for transmission according to its class.
packet_flow: Ingress -> Classification (match fields) -> Marking (e.g. DSCP set) -> Forwarding decision -> Queuing/Scheduling -> Egress, prioritized per its assigned class.
header_fields: IPv4 ToS byte: IP Precedence (3 bits) or DSCP (6 bits).
best_practices: Set the Trust Boundary as close to the traffic source as possible (ingress switch port or the IP phone itself).
Use Policing on ingress and Shaping on egress, matching each tool's intended location.
interview_questions: Explain the difference between Policing and Shaping.
Why does CoS get lost when a packet crosses a router?
What are the voice traffic requirements for delay, jitter, and loss?
exam_traps: Forgetting that CoS (802.1Q, Layer 2) does not survive a Layer 3 hop — DSCP (IP header) does.
enterprise_notes: Voice: <150ms delay, <30ms jitter, <1% loss. Video: 384Kbps-20+Mbps bandwidth, 200-400ms delay, 30-50ms jitter, 0.1-1% loss. EF (DSCP 46) is standard marking for voice.
